scenario: Effect-ts (functional effect system) vs vanilla async/await for client error handling in a fintech app.
context: Financial transactions, multiple failure modes (network, validation, business). Need uniform error handling.
alex_initial_position: Effect-ts. Encodes errors in types, forces handling, composable.
sam_initial_position: Vanilla async/await with discriminated union results. Effect-ts learning curve is real.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Effect-ts catches unhandled errors at compile time. For fintech, every error path matters.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Discriminated unions (Result<T, E>) achieve the same without library lock-in.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Result types in TS lack composition - chaining 5 Results is verbose. Effect-ts has bind/map/flatMap.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For fintech with 5 engineers, Effect-ts learning curve > value. Stick with Result + helpers.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: For 20+ engineers in finance domain, Effect-ts pays off.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed. Effect-ts for large teams in domain-rich areas. Result types otherwise.

context_dependent_answer: 5 engineers: Result types. 20+ engineers in domain-rich: Effect-ts. JS-only teams: stick with try/catch.
decision_framework: debate-126: judge the state management architecture debate in this scenario: Effect-ts (functional effect system) vs vanilla async/await for client error handling in a fintech app.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors throw and forget. Senior insight: errors are part of the type. Encode them. Failed-silently bugs are the worst kind.

scenario: useImperativeHandle for exposing methods (focus, scroll) to parent. Anti-pattern or escape hatch?
context: Custom Input that parent needs to focus programmatically.
alex_initial_position: useImperativeHandle is fine for narrow imperative APIs (focus, scrollTo). Built-in for this purpose.
sam_initial_position: Avoid. Use props-driven state (autoFocus prop, scrollTo prop with value change).

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Programmatic focus from an event handler can't be expressed as props. Need imperative.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Props with a 'trigger' value (Date.now()) re-runs effect → focuses. Declarative.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Trigger pattern is awkward (passing Date.now()). useImperativeHandle is cleaner.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Imperative API encourages misuse. Parent reaches into child internals.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Narrow API (focus only) doesn't expose internals. It's the same as <input>.focus().

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Granted for narrow. useImperativeHandle for focus/scrollTo only.

where_they_disagree: Trigger pattern vs imperative for focus
context_dependent_answer: Focus/scroll: useImperativeHandle narrow. State changes: props. Complex APIs: split component.
decision_framework: debate-127: judge the component composition patterns debate in this scenario: useImperativeHandle for exposing methods (focus, scroll) to parent. Anti-pattern or escape hatch?. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors avoid useImperativeHandle as 'unidiomatic'. Senior insight: it exists for imperative needs (focus). Keep narrow.

scenario: TanStack Form vs React Hook Form for new project. TanStack Form is newer, more headless.
context: New project, team prefers TanStack ecosystem (already uses TanStack Query, Router).
alex_initial_position: TanStack Form. Ecosystem fit. Better TypeScript than RHF.
sam_initial_position: RHF. Mature, larger community, battle-tested.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: TanStack Form's TS inference is best-in-class. Field-level reactivity built in.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: RHF has 40K stars vs TanStack Form's few thousand. Community matters for hiring.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: TanStack Form's API is closer to React's mental model. Less 'register' magic.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: 'register' is a known pattern. New devs learn it.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: For team already on TanStack ecosystem, consistency wins.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Granted for TanStack-shop. RHF for general.

context_dependent_answer: TanStack ecosystem: TanStack Form. General/RHF muscle memory: RHF. Either for greenfield.
decision_framework: debate-128: judge the form library choices debate in this scenario: TanStack Form vs React Hook Form for new project. TanStack Form is newer, more headless.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors pick by stars. Senior insight: ecosystem fit reduces friction. Consistency within a stack matters.

scenario: MSW (Mock Service Worker) vs jest.mock vs in-component mocks for tests.
context: Team's tests mix patterns. Unclear standard.
alex_initial_position: MSW. Service worker intercepts at network layer. Same code as production.
sam_initial_position: MSW for integration, jest.mock for unit (mocking specific module).

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: MSW gives one mock pattern across unit and integration. Less mental load.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For unit tests of a hook that calls fetch, jest.mock('node:fetch') is faster setup.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: MSW setup is once per project. Reuse across tests.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: jest.mock is per-test. Fine for isolated unit.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Granted. MSW for integration where realistic mocking matters. jest.mock for module-level unit.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed.

context_dependent_answer: Integration: MSW. Unit testing module: jest.mock. Network-level test: MSW.
decision_framework: debate-129: judge the data fetching strategy debate in this scenario: MSW (Mock Service Worker) vs jest.mock vs in-component mocks for tests.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors mock per-component. Senior insight: network-level mocking (MSW) keeps tests realistic. Component code stays unaware of being tested.

scenario: CSS-in-JS runtime (Emotion, styled-components) vs zero-runtime (Tailwind, Vanilla Extract). Which for product app?
context: App with dynamic theming, runtime style computation needed.
alex_initial_position: Zero-runtime. Runtime CSS-in-JS has measurable perf cost. Tailwind + CSS variables for dynamic.
sam_initial_position: Emotion for genuinely dynamic styles. CSS variables can't compute (string concatenation, conditional sets).

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: CSS variables + clamp() handle most dynamic cases. JS can set variables.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For truly arbitrary user-defined styles (theme editor where user picks anything), runtime CSS-in-JS shines.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Even user-defined themes can use variables - the theme is just values to set on root.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For style derivation (mix two user colors), variable + color-mix() works. For complex layout derivations, runtime CSS-in-JS easier.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Layout derivations are rare. Cost-benefit favors zero-runtime.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Granted. Zero-runtime default. Emotion only for niche.

context_dependent_answer: Most apps: Tailwind v4 + CSS variables. Theme editors with user-defined: hybrid (Emotion for derived). Marketing: Tailwind only.
decision_framework: debate-130: judge the styling approach wars debate in this scenario: CSS-in-JS runtime (Emotion, styled-components) vs zero-runtime (Tailwind, Vanilla Extract). Which for product app?. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors choose CSS-in-JS for 'theming'. Senior insight: theming is variable values, not generated stylesheets. CSS variables handle 99% of theming.

scenario: Reanimated (React Native) vs CSS for animations. Wait, web context: WAAPI vs Framer Motion.
context: Need scroll-driven animations.
alex_initial_position: WAAPI for scroll-driven. New scroll() and view() timelines.
sam_initial_position: Framer Motion's useScroll hook. Battle-tested.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: WAAPI's scroll-timeline runs on compositor. No JS callback per scroll event.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: WAAPI scroll-timeline browser support gaps. Framer Motion is universal.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Chrome supports it. Progressive enhancement: WAAPI with Framer Motion fallback.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Two implementations to maintain.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: For perf-critical, worth it. For nice-to-have, Framer Motion only.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed.

context_dependent_answer: Perf-critical scroll: WAAPI + fallback. General: Framer Motion. Simple: CSS scroll-snap.
decision_framework: debate-131: judge the animation library decisions debate in this scenario: Reanimated (React Native) vs CSS for animations. Wait, web context: WAAPI vs Framer Motion.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors hook scroll listeners. Senior insight: scroll handlers run on main thread, jank. WAAPI scroll-timeline is compositor-native.

scenario: Component testing: shallow vs mount. Shallow renders one level; mount renders full tree.
context: Component library tests.
alex_initial_position: Mount (RTL approach). Tests behavior, not structure. Shallow tests implementation.
sam_initial_position: Mount for behavior, shallow for snapshot-style structure.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Shallow tests are brittle. Component change requires shallow update.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Mount is slower (rendering full tree).

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: With jsdom mount is ~5ms per test. Trivial.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed. Mount everywhere. Snapshot only specific paths if needed.

context_dependent_answer: Component testing: mount with RTL. Snapshot: only for stable components.
decision_framework: debate-132: judge the testing philosophy debate in this scenario: Component testing: shallow vs mount. Shallow renders one level; mount renders full tree.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors love shallow for speed. Senior insight: tests need to reflect user experience. Mount > shallow for confidence.

scenario: 'as const' vs explicit literal types for derived constants.
context: Array of options used as discriminator values.
alex_initial_position: 'as const'. Concise, derives literal types.
sam_initial_position: Explicit literal types. Self-documenting.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: 'as const' derives types from data. Less duplication.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Explicit types are intentional. Reader sees the contract.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Hover-over 'as const' shows the inferred type.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For public API, explicit. For internal, 'as const'.

context_dependent_answer: Internal data: as const. Public APIs: explicit. Constants used cross-file: explicit.
decision_framework: debate-133: judge the typescript strictness debate in this scenario: 'as const' vs explicit literal types for derived constants.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors write type Role = 'admin' | 'editor' | 'viewer'; const ROLES = ['admin', 'editor', 'viewer']; Senior insight: one source. 'as const' derives the type.

scenario: Worker offloading: when is moving work to Web Worker worth the complexity?
context: Heavy computation (parsing CSV, image processing).
alex_initial_position: Worker for >100ms work. Main thread should be sub-frame.
sam_initial_position: Worker for >500ms work. Below that, overhead exceeds benefit.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: 100ms blocks user interaction. INP suffers.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Worker postMessage overhead is 10-30ms. For 100ms work, 30% overhead.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Comlink + transferable objects reduce overhead. Plus worker frees main for paint.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For 50-100ms work, requestIdleCallback to defer. No worker needed.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Granted. >200ms = worker. 50-200ms = idle or split. <50ms = inline.

context_dependent_answer: >200ms work: worker. 50-200ms: idle scheduling. <50ms: inline. Always: profile real workload.
decision_framework: debate-134: judge the performance vs dx tradeoffs debate in this scenario: Worker offloading: when is moving work to Web Worker worth the complexity?. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors run everything on main. Senior insight: workers exist for heavy work. INP requires sub-frame main thread.

scenario: Webpack to Vite migration timing. When to migrate from a working Webpack setup?
context: Mature Webpack codebase, dev startup is slow, team productive but frustrated.
alex_initial_position: Migrate when dev startup >30s. Vite gives 10x dev speed.
sam_initial_position: Migrate when other forcing function (major Webpack upgrade, plugin EOL). Risk of subtle bugs.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Dev speed is multiplier. 30s startup × 50 starts/day = 25 min wasted per dev.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Migration risk: subtle env differences, edge cases. Real cost.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Risk bounded by testing. Productivity ongoing.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Rspack as middle ground - drop-in Webpack replacement with Rust speed.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Rspack is right intermediate. Migrate to Vite later if appetite.

context_dependent_answer: Slow dev + appetite: Rspack now, Vite later. Stable + no appetite: stay. New project: Vite.
decision_framework: debate-135: judge the build tool choices debate in this scenario: Webpack to Vite migration timing. When to migrate from a working Webpack setup?. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors migrate for hype. Senior insight: migrations have cost. Need a reason (slow, EOL plugin, etc.).

scenario: Atomic design (atoms/molecules/organisms) vs flat structure for component library.
context: 50-component library.
alex_initial_position: Flat. Atomic design hierarchy is artificial. Just structure by responsibility.
sam_initial_position: Atomic. Maintains discipline about composition.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Atomic design is a Brad Frost thing. Real teams struggle with 'is this molecule or organism'.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Discipline is the point. Boundaries force thought.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Forced thought on artificial categories. Better: 'Button vs Card vs DataTable' - by use case.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Use-case is fine but doesn't enforce composition discipline.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Compose via React; structure is just folders. Folders don't enforce composition.

where_they_agree: Folder structure is convention not enforcement
context_dependent_answer: 50 components: flat by domain (forms/, data-display/, navigation/, feedback/). Atomic design optional.

code_examples.code:
src/components/
  forms/
    Button.tsx
    Input.tsx
    Field.tsx
  data-display/
    Card.tsx
    DataTable.tsx
    List.tsx
  navigation/
    Tabs.tsx
    Breadcrumb.tsx
    Sidebar.tsx
  feedback/
    Alert.tsx
    Toast.tsx
    Modal.tsx

decision_framework: debate-136: judge the component library strategy debate in this scenario: Atomic design (atoms/molecules/organisms) vs flat structure for component library.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors follow atomic design dogmatically. Senior insight: folder structure is convention. What matters is composability of the components themselves.

scenario: Circuit breakers for client-side: should frontend stop retrying after N failures?
context: App calls flaky third-party service. Currently retries indefinitely on error.
alex_initial_position: Circuit breaker. After 5 failures, stop for 60s. Resume after backoff.
sam_initial_position: Exponential backoff is sufficient. Circuit breakers are server-pattern.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Exponential backoff still attempts requests. Circuit open = no attempts.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Backoff to large interval (5 min) ≈ open circuit.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Backoff per-request; circuit shared across requests. New request when circuit open fails immediately, no network call.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For frontend, request frequency is low. Backoff suffices.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: For app with 30 widgets each calling the same service, circuit breaker is cheaper.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Granted for shared-service case. Backoff for one-off.

context_dependent_answer: Shared service used by many components: circuit breaker. One-off call: backoff. Critical path: fail fast.
decision_framework: debate-137: judge the error boundary strategy debate in this scenario: Circuit breakers for client-side: should frontend stop retrying after N failures?. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors retry indefinitely. Senior insight: indefinite retry overloads failing service AND wastes user resources. Cap retries with circuit or backoff.

scenario: Dynamic imports without retry on chunk-load failure. Browser cached old chunk hash, new deploy has new hash. User's cached HTML references old hash → 404.
context: App with frequent deploys.
alex_initial_position: Add chunk-load retry. On failure, fetch new HTML, retry import.
sam_initial_position: Force full page reload on chunk-load failure. Don't try to recover in JS.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Reload loses user state. Retry transparent.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Retry doesn't solve underlying: cached HTML points to chunk that no longer exists. Need new HTML.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Fetch latest index.html, parse manifest, retry import with new hash.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That's complex. Simpler: prompt user to reload.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Prompt is friction. Automatic recovery via manifest re-fetch works.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Granted automatic better. Implement manifest-based recovery.

context_dependent_answer: Frequent deploys: manifest-based retry. Rare deploys: reload acceptable. SPA without router: handle in error boundary.
decision_framework: debate-138: judge the code splitting philosophy debate in this scenario: Dynamic imports without retry on chunk-load failure. Browser cached old chunk hash, new deploy has new hash. User's cached HTML references old hash → 404.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors don't handle chunk-load errors. Senior insight: deploys with cached HTML are a real production failure mode. Plan recovery.

scenario: Skip-to-content link: visible always vs visible on focus only.
context: Marketing site, design wants minimal UI.
alex_initial_position: Visible on focus. Keyboard users see it; mouse users don't. Cleanest.
sam_initial_position: Always visible (subtle). Some keyboard users miss focus-only.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Focus-only is the standard pattern. Tab → skip link appears.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Some users tab from URL bar. Focus on link may not be triggered properly.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Modern browsers handle that. Tab from URL → focus first focusable in body.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Granted. Focus-only with proper styling.

context_dependent_answer: Standard: focus-only skip link. High-AT-usage app: consider always-visible.
decision_framework: debate-139: judge the accessibility tradeoffs debate in this scenario: Skip-to-content link: visible always vs visible on focus only.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors skip the skip link. Senior insight: keyboard users navigate every link. Skip-to-content is non-optional for accessibility.

scenario: Server-side state vs client-side state in modern apps. Where should 'logged in user' live?
context: Auth + user profile data.
alex_initial_position: Server state via React Query. Treat as cached server state.
sam_initial_position: Hybrid - server state for profile, client store for session token.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: All server-derived data should be in React Query. Token is fetched from cookie, profile is fetched from API. Both are server-state.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Token has different lifecycle - sync access during render needed (auth headers). React Query is async.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Token in cookie - browser sends automatically. No JS state needed.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Bearer tokens for cross-origin APIs. Need to read from JS.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Bearer tokens in memory + refresh via cookie. Memory state for current request, server-state for refresh.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed. Bearer in memory, refresh server-state.

context_dependent_answer: Cookie auth: profile in React Query. Bearer: token in memory + refresh server-state.
decision_framework: debate-140: judge the frontend architecture patterns debate in this scenario: Server-side state vs client-side state in modern apps. Where should 'logged in user' live?. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors put user in Zustand. Senior insight: user is server-state. Treating it as client state creates sync bugs.

scenario: Optimistic updates with concurrent edits. Two users edit same item simultaneously. How does the client handle the merge?
context: Multi-user app, optimistic mutations, last-write-wins server.
alex_initial_position: Client tracks user-id of last edit, server checks. Reject if stale.
sam_initial_position: CRDT for conflict-free merge. Avoid stale-write rejections.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Optimistic concurrency control (version number) is simpler than CRDT.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: User rejection on stale write loses their work. CRDT preserves both.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: For low-conflict scenarios (rare concurrent edits), OCC is fine.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For collaborative apps (high conflict), CRDT.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Agreed. OCC for low-conflict, CRDT for collaborative.

context_dependent_answer: Low conflict: OCC. Collaborative: CRDT. Server-of-truth: server arbitrates.
decision_framework: debate-141: judge the state management architecture debate in this scenario: Optimistic updates with concurrent edits. Two users edit same item simultaneously. How does the client handle the merge?. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors ignore conflicts. Senior insight: concurrent edits happen. Plan conflict handling explicitly.

scenario: Variant API: variants object (cva) vs boolean props (isPrimary, isLarge).
context: Design system Button.
alex_initial_position: Variants object (cva). Explicit variant + size as enums.
sam_initial_position: Boolean props. Simpler at call site.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Boolean props allow combinatorial invalid states (isPrimary + isDanger).

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Variants force one choice. Restrictive.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Restrictive is good. Variants prevent invalid states.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For some cases (loading + disabled), booleans coexist.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: loading and disabled are independent, not variants. Variant for visual style, independent props for state.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed. Variants for style, booleans for independent state.

context_dependent_answer: Visual style: variants. Independent state: booleans.
decision_framework: debate-142: judge the component composition patterns debate in this scenario: Variant API: variants object (cva) vs boolean props (isPrimary, isLarge).. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors mix variants and state as booleans. Senior insight: visual styles are mutually exclusive (one variant at a time); state is independent. Different props for different axes.

scenario: Async field validation: throttle vs debounce.
context: Username availability check on type.
alex_initial_position: Debounce. User stops typing, check once.
sam_initial_position: Throttle. Continuous feedback during typing.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Throttle fires while typing. Wasted requests during 'admi' → 'admin'.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Faster feedback. User sees 'taken' on first match.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: User wants final result after typing. Debounce with 400ms.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Granted. Debounce.

context_dependent_answer: Async validation: debounce 400ms + AbortController for cancellation.
decision_framework: debate-143: judge the form library choices debate in this scenario: Async field validation: throttle vs debounce.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors use throttle thinking it's faster. Senior insight: debounce waits for user pause. Throttle is for continuous signals (scroll, mouse).

scenario: Streaming SSR (defer + Suspense) vs full SSR. When does streaming pay off?
context: App with slow upstream services (recommendation engine takes 800ms).
alex_initial_position: Streaming SSR. Send fast HTML first, stream slow parts.
sam_initial_position: Streaming for slow upstreams. Full SSR fine for fast upstreams.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Even for fast upstreams, streaming lets client start parsing earlier.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For <100ms total, streaming overhead exceeds benefit.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: True at fast end. For 800ms recs, streaming clearly wins.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed.

where_they_agree: Both can coexist on a page
context_dependent_answer: Slow upstreams (>300ms): streaming. Fast: full SSR. Mixed page: streaming for slow widgets.
decision_framework: debate-144: judge the data fetching strategy debate in this scenario: Streaming SSR (defer + Suspense) vs full SSR. When does streaming pay off?. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors do full SSR everywhere. Senior insight: page LCP is bounded by slowest upstream. Streaming bypasses that.

scenario: Co-located CSS modules vs Tailwind for a 100-page app.
context: Team familiar with CSS Modules. Considering Tailwind for new development.
alex_initial_position: Tailwind. Faster iteration, no naming overhead.
sam_initial_position: CSS Modules. Familiar, scoped, no class soup.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Tailwind eliminates 'what to name this class' decision fatigue.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Class strings in JSX become long. Readability degrades.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Multiline className with formatters. Or extract to component.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Component extraction is forced abstraction.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Extraction is good - components are the abstraction unit.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For CSS Modules, BEM-naming and scoped css work. Familiar.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: For new development, Tailwind. Migrate slowly.

context_dependent_answer: New project: Tailwind. Existing CSS Modules: continue. Migration: incremental.
decision_framework: debate-145: judge the styling approach wars debate in this scenario: Co-located CSS modules vs Tailwind for a 100-page app.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors fight new tooling. Senior insight: pick the tooling for the next 5 years, not the past 5.

scenario: GSAP vs Framer Motion for complex timeline animations.
context: Marketing hero with synchronized text-image-svg animation.
alex_initial_position: GSAP. Timeline API is purpose-built for this.
sam_initial_position: Framer Motion. React-native.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: GSAP timeline for synchronized choreography is unmatched.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Framer Motion's stagger + variants achieve similar.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: For complex sequences (3+ elements synchronized over 5s), GSAP simpler.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: GSAP is licensed (paid for some uses).

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: GSAP free for non-resale. Commercial license for plugins.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Granted for hero animations: GSAP. Rest of app: Framer Motion.

context_dependent_answer: Complex marketing animations: GSAP. App animations: Framer Motion.
decision_framework: debate-146: judge the animation library decisions debate in this scenario: GSAP vs Framer Motion for complex timeline animations.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors use one tool. Senior insight: animation tooling specializes. GSAP for choreography, Framer for component animation.

scenario: Visual regression testing: Chromatic vs Percy vs Playwright + image diff.
context: Component library, ~200 visual scenarios.
alex_initial_position: Chromatic. Storybook integration, baseline management.
sam_initial_position: Playwright + image diff. Free, controllable.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Chromatic CI integration handles baseline approval flow. Building this is weeks.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Playwright + git LFS for baseline storage. Setup once.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: DIY misses cross-browser baselines. Chromatic handles Chrome/Firefox/Safari.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For component lib, one browser baseline often sufficient.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: For shipping to product teams, cross-browser is required.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Granted. Chromatic for component lib.

context_dependent_answer: Component lib: Chromatic. App E2E: Playwright + image diff for specific screens.
decision_framework: debate-147: judge the testing philosophy debate in this scenario: Visual regression testing: Chromatic vs Percy vs Playwright + image diff.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors skip visual regression. Senior insight: CSS bugs are silent. Visual regression is the only catch for 'looks slightly different in Safari'.

scenario: Discriminated union state machines vs boolean flags for component state.
context: Modal with loading, error, success states.
alex_initial_position: Discriminated union. Type-level mutual exclusion.
sam_initial_position: Boolean flags (isLoading, error). Familiar.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Booleans allow invalid combinations (isLoading + error). Discriminated union prevents.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Discipline prevents bad combinations. Booleans simpler.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Type prevents at compile time. Discipline fails at runtime.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Granted. Union for state machines.

context_dependent_answer: Multi-state components: discriminated union. Independent flags: booleans.
decision_framework: debate-148: judge the typescript strictness debate in this scenario: Discriminated union state machines vs boolean flags for component state.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors use booleans for everything. Senior insight: mutually exclusive states need union types. Booleans encode 4 states (TT/TF/FT/FF) when you want 1 of N.

scenario: Pre-rendering (SSG) vs server-rendering (SSR) for product pages.
context: E-commerce site, 10K products, prices update daily.
alex_initial_position: SSG with ISR. Pre-render at build, revalidate periodically.
sam_initial_position: SSR. Fresh data on every request.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: SSG serves from CDN. Sub-100ms TTFB globally.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Stale prices on SSG. Need invalidation on price change.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: ISR with 1-hour revalidate handles daily price updates. Plus on-demand revalidation via webhook.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Granted. ISR + on-demand for product pages.

context_dependent_answer: E-commerce: ISR. News/feeds: SSR. Static marketing: pure SSG.
decision_framework: debate-149: judge the performance vs dx tradeoffs debate in this scenario: Pre-rendering (SSG) vs server-rendering (SSR) for product pages.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors pick SSR for 'freshness'. Senior insight: ISR is the modern default - static performance with freshness on update.

scenario: Bun vs Node for build/dev tooling. Bun is faster but newer.
context: New project, can pick either.
alex_initial_position: Bun. Faster install, faster scripts, integrated tooling.
sam_initial_position: Node. Mature, all libraries tested on it.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Bun's package install is 10-30x faster than npm.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Some packages don't work on Bun. Compat is a risk.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Compat is 95%+. Edge cases workaroundable.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: 95% means 1 in 20 packages breaks. Costly when hit.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: For new projects with controlled deps, low risk.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Production runtime: still Node. Bun for dev only.

context_dependent_answer: Greenfield with appetite: Bun for dev. Conservative or production runtime: Node.
decision_framework: debate-150: judge the build tool choices debate in this scenario: Bun vs Node for build/dev tooling. Bun is faster but newer.. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors don't try new tools. Senior insight: tooling improves. Bun's speed is real. Evaluate for greenfield.
